---
title: "Create a pipeline"
description: "Chain components into a VDSL3 Nextflow workflow"
order: 50
---

A pipeline chains components into a workflow. OpenPipeline workflows are **VDSL3
pipelines**, built with [Viash](https://viash.io) from a `config.vsh.yaml` plus a
`main.nf`. The mechanics of VDSL3, channels, and the `.run()` operator are
covered in the [Viash guide](https://viash.io/guide/); this page covers the
OpenPipeline conventions and walks through a real workflow.

## OpenPipeline conventions

- **Layout.** A workflow lives in `src/workflows/<category>/<name>/`, with the
  category following the analysis stage, such as `rna`, `integration`,
  `annotation`, or `multiomics`. It holds a `config.vsh.yaml`, a `main.nf` (the
  `run_wf` workflow), a `nextflow.config`, a `test.nf`, and, by convention, an
  `integration_test.sh`.
- **State-based channels.** Data flows as `[id, state]` tuples. Components read
  from the state with `fromState` and write back with `toState`. The
  [data-state view](../concepts/data-state.qmd) explains the lens behind this.
- **Declare dependencies.** List every component or sub-workflow used in `main.nf`
  under `dependencies` in the config. Viash resolves and compiles the modules at
  build time, so imports are never written by hand.
- **Dataflow utilities.** Reuse `dataflow/concatenate_h5mu`, `split_modalities`,
  `merge`, and `split_h5mu` for common channel manipulations instead of
  reimplementing them.
- **Resource labels.** Assign CPU and memory with
  [resource labels](../guides/tune-resources.qmd) on each component; the workflow
  inherits them.

## Anatomy of a workflow

A workflow folder pairs the config with a Nextflow script and its tests:

```
src/workflows/rna/log_normalize/
├── config.vsh.yaml       # arguments + dependencies
├── main.nf               # the run_wf workflow
├── nextflow.config
├── test.nf               # the test workflow
└── integration_test.sh   # end-to-end run against test data
```

### The config

The config groups the pipeline's arguments and, crucially, lists its
`dependencies`. Trimmed from the real
[`workflows/rna/log_normalize`](https://github.com/openpipelines-bio/openpipeline/blob/main/src/workflows/rna/log_normalize/config.vsh.yaml):

```yaml
name: "log_normalize"
namespace: "workflows/rna"
description: "Normalization and log-transformation of raw count data."
argument_groups:
  - name: "Inputs"
    arguments:
      - name: "--input"
        type: file
        required: true
      - name: "--modality"
        type: string
        default: "rna"
  - name: "Output"
    arguments:
      - name: "--output"
        type: file
        direction: output
        required: true
dependencies:
  - name: transform/normalize_total
  - name: transform/log1p
  - name: transform/delete_layer
resources:
  - type: nextflow_script
    path: main.nf
    entrypoint: run_wf
runners:
  - type: nextflow
```

Each entry under `dependencies` is a component (or sub-workflow) the pipeline
calls. Because they are declared here, `viash ns build` compiles them into the
`target/` modules that `main.nf` uses, so you never write `include` statements
yourself.

### The main workflow

`main.nf` defines `run_wf`, a chain of `.run()` calls on the declared
dependencies. Each component pulls its inputs from the state with `fromState` and
writes results back with `toState`:

```groovy
workflow run_wf {
  take:
  input_ch

  main:
  output_ch = input_ch
    | normalize_total.run(
        fromState: ["input": "input", "modality": "modality"],
        args:      ["output_layer": "normalized"],
        toState:   ["input": "output"]
      )
    | log1p.run(
        fromState: ["input": "input", "modality": "modality"],
        args:      ["input_layer": "normalized"],
        toState:   ["input": "output"]
      )
    | setState(["output"])

  emit:
  output_ch
}
```

Data enters as `[id, state]` tuples, each step reads the fields it needs from the
state and merges its output back, and `setState` selects the final fields to
emit. This is the [data-state model](../concepts/data-state.qmd) in practice.

## Build and run

A workflow needs its dependencies compiled first: run `viash ns build`, then
`nextflow run`. See [Build and test locally](development-workflow.qmd) for the
details.

## Publishing

When your workflow is ready, open a pull request and follow
[Open a pull request](pull-requests.qmd). Once merged, Viash Hub builds it into
the package and publishes it with the next release.
